item.title: Women Lead Here: Playing the long game
item.description: Pursuing gender equity can be hard, slow work at the best of times — and these are not that. Here’s what corporate Canada can learn from the companies that stick to it

item.title: Editor’s Note: Timing is everything
item.description: In this edition, we offer readers timely stories that also give some measure of escape from the constant stream of disheartening headlines

item.title: Women Lead Here
item.description: Ranking the Top Companies for Women in Executive Leadership

item.title: Oaktree private credit fund to meet 8.5% redemption requests in full
item.description: Oaktree’s parent Brookfield will purchase 1.7% of shares to help meet requests

item.title: China launches probes into U.S. trade practices in response to Trump’s tariff moves
item.description: Move restricts the entry of Chinese products to the U.S. and U.S. exports of high-tech products to China

item.title: Pernod Ricard and Jack Daniel’s owner in talks to combine operations
item.description: Discussions under way with Brown-Forman to create premium alcohol group as sector battles subdued demand

item.title: Canada nears free-trade deal with Mercosur block, sources say
item.description: Officials say agreement could be reached as early as September

item.title: Business Brief: Artificial-intelligence superusers automate their lives
item.description: But there are bugs and security risks with AI agents, too

item.title: Trump’s Iran war is going to end badly, unless it ends worse
item.description: Despite the damage that thousands of air strikes have inflicted on Iran, it is now Trump who is crying uncle

item.title: Senior women in accounting make 30 per cent less than men
item.description: The post Senior women in accounting make 30 per cent less than men appeared first on The Logic .

item.title: Israel launches strikes on Iran with no sign of diplomatic breakthrough
item.description: Trump claims talks with Tehran going well, extends deadline to open the Strait of Hormuz

item.description: Test your recollection of the top stories of the week ending March 27

item.title: Italy probes Sephora over fears marketing is driving ‘cosmeticorexia’ in girls
item.description: Competition regulator claims retailer and Benefit Cosmetics encourage purchases through ‘covert’ social media marketing

item.title: Lloyds IT glitch exposed thousands of customer accounts
item.description: MPs on Treasury committee reveal bank’s software update caused March incident

item.title: For these busy parents and professionals, AI agents are the personal assistants of their dreams
item.description: OpenClaw is capable of everything from hunting down baby gear to prioritizing meetings, offering a look at where artificial intelligence might go next

item.title: Companies in carbon-intensive industries pay higher interest rates on their bonds, study shows
item.description: Climate advocacy group I4PC urges Canadian Securities Administrators to resume working on mandatory climate disclosure rules

item.title: Canadian investments in Cuba at risk as Trump muses about invasion
item.description: Ottawa should step up efforts to defend the interests of Canadian companies that are already exposed to the country

item.title: Private credit’s stale pricing problem
item.description: More frequent asset valuations can boost market resilience and investor confidence

item.title: Corporate bonds are the new stocks
item.description: Plus, the defence of private credit

item.title: Nato allies to train with Ukraine as Europe seeks closer defence ties
item.description: Also in this newsletter: Europe races to fund fusion

item.title: Everything you always wanted to know about credit (but were afraid to ask)
item.description: To pay or not to pay, that is the question

item.title: Trump extends Iran talks deadline after sell-off on Wall Street
item.description: US stocks post worst day since Middle East crisis began as fresh inflation fears send Treasury yields higher

item.title: FT Wealth: March
item.description: In this edition: Charles Spencer asks what it’s like to live in one of Sir John Vanbrugh’s masterpieces; Clare Maurice examines the tax, visa and estate-planning pitfalls US citizens should weigh before relocating to the UK; Bryce Elder argues that One Hyde Park’s forbidding exclusivity is not a flaw but the very essence of its appeal.

item.title: Corebridge looks to crash Apollo’s party
item.description: Insurers Equitable and Corebridge are set to merge to create a $22bn life insurance giant

item.title: UK watchdog hits Bank of London with new client freeze
item.description: Lossmaking fintech was fined £2mn for misleading regulators about its capital position

item.title: The new Disney CEO’s horrible first week
item.description: Josh D’Amaro faces crises across the entertainment giant’s empire

item.title: The global food crisis unleashed by the war
item.description: From Minnesota to Punjab, fertiliser costs are up and harvests are set to be hit

item.title: Molecules are taking their revenge on services
item.description: Capital-intensive industries matter in the age of the Halo trade Gillian Tett

item.title: Ships forgo cargo to carry fuel as Iran war sends prices soaring
item.description: Fuel costs for global shipping industry are up nearly $5bn since conflict began

item.title: UAE pushes for international force to reopen Hormuz
item.description: Abu Dhabi is hardening its stance as it suffers from Iran’s retaliation to US-Israeli war

item.title: Private markets are developing their own Magnificent Seven
item.description: Investors are splashing cash at record rates, but the market has been getting narrower

item.title: How Iran is seeking to cash in on Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Tehran suggests system of charging fees and limiting passage to ‘non-hostile’ ships could endure beyond the war

item.title: German ‘city of peace’ wrestles with weapons pivot to save VW jobs
item.description: Converting Osnabrück factory to make Iron Dome components may be ‘moonshot’ for embattled carmaker

item.title: Echoes of history: what the oil shock means for your money
item.description: As central bankers tread the line between controlling inflation and avoiding economic stagnation, investors face tough decisions

item.title: How Italy’s oldest bank turned on its saviour CEO
item.description: Monte dei Paschi di Siena’s board stripped Luigi Lovaglio of his powers, setting up a fight for its leadership

item.title: SocGen ditches ‘shadow’ trading floors in favour of work from home
item.description: French bank decides traders can operate from home if main sites are out of action

item.title: Lessons for rich families from a private banker
item.description: The secret to sustaining generational wealth isn’t financial wizardry but long-term character

item.description: Read the full issue, exclusively for subscribers

item.title: BCE selling land mobile radio network business to Motorola subsidiary
item.description: Sale worth $675-million part of plan to sell off $7-billion in assets to pay down debt

item.title: Judge temporarily blocks Pentagon from labelling Anthropic a supply chain risk
item.description: Federal judge also blocks enforcement of Trump’s social media directive ordering all federal agencies to stop using Anthropic and its chatbot Claude

item.title: Government launches inquiry into imports of certain vegetables
item.description: Industry asked federal government to give peas, corn and other veggies a chance as imports have risen

item.description: President delays possible attack on energy plants until April 6

item.description: The world’s largest stablecoin issuer also brings on PwC to ready internal systems

item.title: McCain Foods heir sues French fry maker after failing to sell her $1-billion-plus stake
item.description: Eleanor McCain’s claim details a year-long fight over the sale of her inheritance

item.title: Trump’s signature will be on all new U.S. paper currency, Treasury announces
item.description: Move would be a first for a sitting president

item.title: Middle East war day 27 as it happened: Donald Trump extends Iran talks deadline after oil price jumps the most in 2 weeks and US stocks hit 6-month low
item.description: US president delays possible attack on Iran’s energy plants until April 6 after bruising day on Wall Street

item.description: It’s been eight years since the Ford government first promised to introduce meaningful tax relief

item.description: Unusually for a merger, the share prices of the two companies barely moved after the deal was announced

item.title: In Air Canada’s French fallout, it is unfairly treated as both a private and public entity
item.description: Language controversy demonstrates how hamstrung the airline is due to obligations from 1988

item.description: Carolyn Rogers says central bank reconsidering how it thinks and talks about inflation after series of economic shocks

item.description: Democratic officials are reassessing targets as concerns grow over energy affordability

item.description: World trade needs the WTO - and so does Canada

item.description: Greater access to private markets without disclosure only shifts risk, and is not a solution to public-market stagnation

item.description: Demand growing for immunoglobulin to address immune deficiencies that can result from cancer treatment
